nativeRange.habitat: Coral and rocky reef ledges, lagoons, and outer reef slopes; tends to shelter beneath overhangs and inside caves during the day, ranging from shallow water to roughly 122 m depth

diet.notes: Wild diet has been documented to include crabs, shrimps, and small fishes. In aquaria, generally accepts meaty foods such as mysis, krill, silversides, and chopped seafood once acclimated. Live foods (ghost shrimp, brine shrimp) are commonly recommended to initiate feeding in newly introduced specimens. Best fed in the evening or after lights-out to match its nocturnal foraging habit.

avoidWith: small fish that fit in mouth

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso; 7279x4853. Tack-sharp full-body in-situ Red Sea shot showing the brilliant pink-red body, large nocturnal eye, and yellow-edged fins. Best diagnostic and quality.
MDC Seamarc Maldives; 3573x2678. Sharp full-body reef shot with vivid orange-red coloration and yellow fins; good color/angle variety versus the primary.
Philippe Bourjon; 1200x923. Sharp full-body shot among staghorn coral showing deep magenta-red flanks and orange-yellow fins; adds a third color variant.

summary: The Scarlet Fin Soldier (Sargocentron spiniferum) is the largest known squirrelfish, an Indo-Pacific nocturnal reef predator with brilliant red coloration, venomous preopercle spines, and a strong preference for cave-rich aquascapes.
careNotes:
The Scarlet Fin Soldier, more widely known as the Sabre Squirrelfish, is a Holocentrid reef fish ranging from the Red Sea and East Africa east to Hawaii and Ducie, north to southern Japan and south to Australia. Wild specimens have been documented up to about 51 cm fork length, making it the largest squirrelfish; aquarium specimens commonly mature around 30–45 cm. It tends to be cryptic and shy in daylight, sheltering beneath ledges and inside caves, and becomes active to forage at night.

Venomous spines: The preopercle (cheek) spine has been reported to be venomous and can inflict a painful wound when handling. Squirrelfish spines are also notorious for snagging in nets, so a soft container or coral net is generally advised when moving specimens. Treating the wound with hot water (as hot as tolerable) to break down the venom and seeking medical attention if pain is severe are commonly recommended responses.

Nocturnal husbandry: This species tends to be most active when room and aquarium lights are dimmed or off. Feeding after lights-out, providing deep caves or overhangs, and accepting that the fish may be hidden much of the day are generally part of keeping this species successfully. Live food (ghost shrimp, small feeder shrimp, krill) is commonly used to initiate feeding before transitioning to frozen meaty foods.

Reef compatibility: The Scarlet Fin Soldier is generally regarded as reef-safe with caution. It tends to leave corals and sessile invertebrates (those permanently attached to the rock or substrate) alone, but will eat any ornamental shrimp, small crabs, or fish small enough to fit in its sizeable mouth. Not suitable for shrimp-and-coral display reefs; works well in fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) or large predator-tolerant reef systems.

Jumping/escape: A well-sealed lid is generally recommended, as startled squirrelfish have been known to jump.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Holocentridae, Holocentriformes), maximum size (51 cm FL, 35 cm TL typical, 2.6 kg), preferred temperature (24.4–29°C, mean 27.8°C), depth range (0–122 m), native range (Indo-Pacific from Red Sea/East Africa to Hawaii/Ducie), lifespan (up to 7 years), diet (crabs, shrimps, small fish), nocturnal behavior, and venomous preopercle spine.

additional.name: Reef App
additional.notes: Confirmed reef-safe-with-caution status, salinity ~1.026, temperature ~26°C, and recommended a much larger 1500 L minimum tank for full adults. Bias-larger tank rule applied: 700 L chosen as a midpoint between retailer (190–265 L) and specialist (1500 L) recommendations.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (salinity 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–12 dKH), captive max size (~12 in / 30 cm), 'Moderate' care level, and non-reef-safe rating tied to predation on small inverts. Stated temperature range (72–78°F) is cooler than FishBase wild preferendum; averaged values used.

additional.name: Reef Guide
additional.notes: Confirmed adult length 30–45 cm, depth range 10–90 m, and Indo-Pacific distribution including Hawaii, Red Sea, Great Barrier Reef, and French Polynesia.
